era_context: The mid-8th century CE spans the height of the Abbasid Caliphate's consolidation of power in the Islamic world, the Tang Dynasty's continued dominance in East Asia, and the Carolingian Empire's expansion under Charlemagne in Western Europe. This is a period of significant cultural, religious, and political transformation across Eurasia, with major trade routes (Silk Road, maritime routes) facilitating exchange. In East Africa and South Asia, regional kingdoms and sultanates were developing complex trade networks.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written sources; archaeological record incomplete)
Pre-Columbian Americas (no written records for this period; archaeological dating often imprecise)
Oceania and Pacific islands (oral traditions not well-preserved in written form)
Northern Europe beyond Frankish realm (sparse documentation)

events.title: Charlemagne's Campaign Against the Saxons Begins
events.description: Charlemagne, King of the Franks, launched the first major military campaign against the Saxon tribes of northern Europe. This campaign was motivated by both territorial expansion and religious conversion efforts. The Saxons, pagan Germanic peoples, resisted Frankish and Christian encroachment. This conflict would continue intermittently for over 30 years, becoming one of the defining military struggles of Charlemagne's reign and resulting in the forced Christianization of Saxony.
key_figures: Widukind (Saxon leader, emerged later in conflict)

sources.name: Einhard, Life of Charlemagne (Vita Karoli Magni)

events.certainty_note: The Royal Frankish Annals, compiled near-contemporaneously, record the initiation of Saxon campaigns in 772 CE, though some sources place initial conflict in 770. The date 770 is traditional in some chronicles but 772 is more widely accepted by modern scholarship.

events.title: Death of Pepin the Short; Charlemagne and Carloman Divide the Frankish Kingdom
events.description: Pepin the Short, King of the Franks and father of Charlemagne, died in 768 CE. Upon his death, the Frankish kingdom was divided between his two sons: Charlemagne received the larger portion (northern and eastern territories), while Carloman received the southern and central regions. This division created significant tension between the brothers and left the kingdom fragmented. The partition would prove unstable and contribute to internal conflict until Carloman's death in 771 CE.
key_figures: Charlemagne (King of the Franks, northern territories)
Carloman (King of the Franks, southern territories)
events.certainty_note: The division occurred in 768 CE upon Pepin's death; by 770 CE, the division was established fact. Contemporary chronicles confirm the arrangement.

events.title: Abbasid Caliphate Consolidation Under al-Mansur
events.region: Islamic Caliphate (Middle East, North Africa, Central Asia)
events.description: The Abbasid Caliphate, which had overthrown the Umayyad Dynasty in 750 CE, continued its consolidation of power under Caliph al-Mansur (r. 754–775 CE). By 770 CE, al-Mansur had established the Abbasid administrative system, suppressed major revolts, and was preparing to found Baghdad as the new capital (founded 762 CE, still under development). The Abbasid period represented a shift toward Persian administrative models and a more centralized bureaucracy compared to the Umayyad system.

sources.name: al-Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings (Tarikh al-Rusul wa-al-Muluk)
Various Islamic chronicles and administrative records

events.certainty_note: The Abbasid consolidation is well-documented in Islamic historical sources, though many were compiled centuries later. The general trajectory and al-Mansur's reign dates are secure.

events.title: Tang Dynasty Stability and Cultural Flourishing in China
events.description: During the reign of Emperor Daizong of Tang (r. 762–779 CE), the Tang Dynasty was in a period of relative stability following the An Lushan Rebellion (755–763 CE). By 770 CE, the dynasty had recovered much of its administrative capacity and was experiencing a cultural renaissance. Poetry, painting, and ceramics flourished. The Silk Road trade was active, facilitating exchange with Central Asia, the Islamic world, and beyond. This period saw significant developments in Buddhist and Daoist thought as well.
key_figures: Du Fu (poet, active during this period)

sources.name: New Book of Tang (Xin Tang Shu)
Records of the Grand Historian continuation (Zizhi Tongjian)

events.certainty_note: Tang Dynasty records are well-preserved in Chinese historical sources. The general cultural and political situation in 770 CE is well-documented.

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional date of 770 CE for the beginning of Charlemagne's Saxon Wars is disputed by modern scholarship. Many historians, following the Royal Frankish Annals more carefully, place the first major campaign in 772 CE rather than 770 CE. The 770 date may conflate earlier skirmishes or diplomatic tensions with the formal military campaign. Additionally, some sources suggest the division of the Frankish kingdom in 768 CE was more stable than later narratives (written after 771) suggest, implying that tensions between Charlemagne and Carloman may have been exaggerated by later chroniclers writing after their conflict.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 770 CE is moderately rich for Western Europe and the Islamic world, drawing primarily on near-contemporary chronicles and later compilations. Chinese sources are abundant and detailed. However, the record is heavily weighted toward political and military events; economic, social, and demographic information is sparse. Sources are often written from court or elite perspectives, potentially obscuring the experiences of common people. For regions outside the major empires (sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, much of Northern Europe), documentation is minimal or absent. The reliability of dating for events in this year varies: Western European and Islamic dates are generally secure; Chinese dates are very reliable; other regions have greater uncertainty.

graph_edges.note: Initial Saxon skirmishes escalated into major military campaigns
The unstable division of the Frankish kingdom led to conflict between brothers
Consolidation continued from the founding of Baghdad
Cultural flourishing occurred in the aftermath of the An Lushan Rebellion
